@story645 

Perhaps you'd adjust according to Member specifications the GNU `sed` lines in the following script.

Furthermore see the note above the `grep` output for the string `hozirontal` in the opening comment.

Owing to other obligations presently my preference would be a Member or other Contributor make the commit.

```
$ cat typos.sh
#!/bin/sh

sed -i "s/Homogenous/Homogeneous/g" matplotlib/galleries/users_explain/axes/constrainedlayout_guide.py
# sed -i "s/compilex/compiled/g" matplotlib/doc/users/prev_whats_new/changelog.rst
sed -i "s/exammple/example/g" matplotlib/lib/matplotlib/sphinxext/plot_directive.py
sed -i "s/functionaliy/functionality/g" matplotlib/doc/api/next_api_changes/removals/20866-JKS.rst
sed -i "s/instaled/installed/g" matplotlib/pyproject.toml
sed -i "s/looger/logger/g" matplotlib/doc/api/prev_api_changes/api_changes_3.1.0.rst
sed -i "s/parmeters/parameters/g" matplotlib/lib/matplotlib/tests/test_colorbar.py
# sed -i "s/pass throughs/passthroughs/g" matplotlib/lib/matplotlib/category.py
sed -i "s/resete/reset/g" matplotlib/lib/matplotlib/tests/test_widgets.py
sed -i "s/secondart/secondary/g" matplotlib/lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py
sed -i "s/tolerence/tolerance/g" matplotlib/doc/api/prev_api_changes/api_changes_3.1.0.rst
$

```

I presume this is intentional.

```
$ grep -nr hozirontal matplotlib
matplotlib/lib/matplotlib/tests/test_text.py:412:        Text(rotation='hozirontal')
$
```

This seems to be bundled customised code.

```
$ grep -nr intialization matplotlib
matplotlib/subprojects/packagefiles/freetype-2.6.1-meson/include/freetype/config/ftoption.h.in:491:  /*   constants will be moved into a struct and additional intialization  */
$
```
ci/schemas/conda-environment.json

```
$ sed -n '331,355p' scipy/doc/source/building/index.rst
    .. tab-set::

      .. tab-item:: Linux
        :sync: linux

        ::

          python -m venv venv
          source venv/bin/activate

      .. tab-item:: macOS
        :sync: macos

        ::

          python -m venv venv
          source venv/bin/activate

      .. tab-item:: Windows
        :sync: windows

        ::

          python -m venv venv
          .\venv\Scripts\activate
$ 
```

doc/source/building/index.rst

```diff
-           .\venv\Scripts\activate
+           .\venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1
```

as per recent output from `pwsh` on Windows

```console
PS C:\Users\bird> python.exe --version
Python 3.12.6
PS C:\Users\bird> python.exe -m venv venv
PS C:\Users\bird> Get-ChildItem venv\Scripts

    Directory: C:\Users\bird\venv\Scripts

Mode                 LastWriteTime         Length Name
----                 -------------         ------ ----
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM           2034 activate
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM            990 activate.bat
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM          26199 Activate.ps1
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM            393 deactivate.bat
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM         108399 pip.exe
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM         108399 pip3.12.exe
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM         108399 pip3.exe
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM         270104 python.exe
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM         258840 pythonw.exe

PS C:\Users\bird>
```

<hr>

Peripheral to the foregoing is noted PowerShell isn't to date a Windows only program.

And the docs aren't specific when commands are provided as to Command Prompt or PowerShell.

The `pyproject.toml` file being updated with some frequency, the following might be considered to implement as being more informative; uppercasing `set` (though cmd.exe is case-insensitive) may emphasise it as a Command Prompt "internal command".

```diff
- # do this instead (which will override this setting)
- # set CIBW_ENVIRONMENT_WINDOWS=PKG_CONFIG_PATH=PWD.replace('\\', '/')
+ # in cmd.exe do this instead (which will override this setting)
+ # SET CIBW_ENVIRONMENT_WINDOWS=PKG_CONFIG_PATH=PWD.replace('\\', '/')
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21576/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21576/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2403033510","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21576#issuecomment-2403033510","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21576","id":2403033510,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6PO2Gm","user":{"login":"musvaage","id":112724366,"node_id":"U_kgDOBrgJjg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/112724366?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage","html_url":"https://github.com/musvaage","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-09T18:39:40Z","updated_at":"2024-10-09T18:39:40Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The table over in the CPython repo has been modified using the executable name only for POSIX.

#include "dtypemeta.h"

/*************************************************************************
 ****************   Implement Buffer Protocol ****************************
 *************************************************************************/

/*************************************************************************
 * PEP 3118 buffer protocol
 *
 * Implementing PEP 3118 is somewhat convoluted because of the desirata:
 *
 * - Don't add new members to ndarray or descr structs, to preserve binary
 *   compatibility. (Also, adding the items is actually not very useful,
 *   since mutability issues prevent an 1 to 1 relationship between arrays
 *   and buffer views.)
 *
 * - Don't use bf_releasebuffer, because it prevents PyArg_ParseTuple("s#", ...
 *   from working. Breaking this would cause several backward compatibility
 *   issues already on Python 2.6.
 *
 * - Behave correctly when array is reshaped in-place, or it's dtype is
 *   altered.
 *
 * The solution taken below is to manually track memory allocated for
 * Py_buffers.
 *************************************************************************/

#include "dtypemeta.h"

/*************************************************************************
 ****************   Implement Buffer Protocol ****************************
 *************************************************************************/

/*************************************************************************
 * PEP 3118 buffer protocol
 *
 * Implementing PEP 3118 is somewhat convoluted because of the desirata:
 *
 * - Don't add new members to ndarray or descr structs, to preserve binary
 *   compatibility. (Also, adding the items is actually not very useful,
 *   since mutability issues prevent an 1 to 1 relationship between arrays
 *   and buffer views.)
 *
 * - Don't use bf_releasebuffer, because it prevents PyArg_ParseTuple("s#", ...
 *   from working. Breaking this would cause several backward compatibility
 *   issues already on Python 2.6.
 *
 * - Behave correctly when array is reshaped in-place, or it's dtype is
 *   altered.
 *
 * The solution taken below is to manually track memory allocated for
 * Py_buffers.
 *************************************************************************/

#include "dtypemeta.h"

/*************************************************************************
 ****************   Implement Buffer Protocol ****************************
 *************************************************************************/

/*************************************************************************
 * PEP 3118 buffer protocol
 *
 * Implementing PEP 3118 is somewhat convoluted because of the desirata:
 *
 * - Don't add new members to ndarray or descr structs, to preserve binary
 *   compatibility. (Also, adding the items is actually not very useful,
 *   since mutability issues prevent an 1 to 1 relationship between arrays
 *   and buffer views.)
 *
 * - Don't use bf_releasebuffer, because it prevents PyArg_ParseTuple("s#", ...
 *   from working. Breaking this would cause several backward compatibility
 *   issues already on Python 2.6.
 *
 * - Behave correctly when array is reshaped in-place, or it's dtype is
 *   altered.
 *
 * The solution taken below is to manually track memory allocated for
 * Py_buffers.
 *************************************************************************/

a few numpy/linalg/lapack_lite directory typos

might be eliminated by regenerating those files with a newer lapack version

however note the following (current release is lapack-3.12.0)

```
$ tail -n 6 numpy/numpy/linalg/lapack_lite/README.rst
The output C files in git use the LAPACK source from the LAPACK_ page, using
version 3.2.2. Unfortunately, newer versions use newer FORTRAN features, which
are increasingly not supported by ``f2c``. As these are found, the patch files
will need to be changed to re-express new constructs with legacy constructs.

.. _LAPACK: https://netlib.org/lapack/index.html
$ 
```

/*************************************************************************
 ****************   Implement Buffer Protocol ****************************

/*************************************************************************
 * PEP 3118 buffer protocol
 *
 * Implementing PEP 3118 is somewhat convoluted because of the desirata:
 *
 * - Don't add new members to ndarray or descr structs, to preserve binary
 *   compatibility. (Also, adding the items is actually not very useful,
 *   since mutability issues prevent an 1 to 1 relationship between arrays
 *   and buffer views.)
 *
 * - Don't use bf_releasebuffer, because it prevents PyArg_ParseTuple("s#", ...
 *   from working. Breaking this would cause several backward compatibility
 *   issues already on Python 2.6.
 *
 * - Behave correctly when array is reshaped in-place, or it's dtype is
 *   altered.
 *
 * The solution taken below is to manually track memory allocated for
 * Py_buffers.
 *************************************************************************/

a few numpy/linalg/lapack_lite directory typos

might be eliminated by regenerating those files with a newer lapack version

however note the following, lapack 3.2.2 was released on June 30, 2010

whereas lapack 3.12.0 (current stable) was released on November 24, 2023

```
$ tail -n 6 numpy/numpy/linalg/lapack_lite/README.rst
The output C files in git use the LAPACK source from the LAPACK_ page, using
version 3.2.2. Unfortunately, newer versions use newer FORTRAN features, which
are increasingly not supported by ``f2c``. As these are found, the patch files
will need to be changed to re-express new constructs with legacy constructs.

.. _LAPACK: https://netlib.org/lapack/index.html
$ 
```

a few numpy/linalg/lapack_lite directory typos

might be eliminated by regenerating those files with a newer lapack version

however note the following, lapack 3.2.2 was released on June 30, 2010

whereas lapack 3.12.0 (current stable) was released on November 24, 2023

```
$ tail -n 6 numpy/numpy/linalg/lapack_lite/README.rst
The output C files in git use the LAPACK source from the LAPACK_ page, using
version 3.2.2. Unfortunately, newer versions use newer FORTRAN features, which
are increasingly not supported by ``f2c``. As these are found, the patch files
will need to be changed to re-express new constructs with legacy constructs.

.. _LAPACK: https://netlib.org/lapack/index.html
$ 
```

/*************************************************************************
 * PEP 3118 buffer protocol
 *
 * Implementing PEP 3118 is somewhat convoluted because of the desirata:
 *
 * - Don't add new members to ndarray or descr structs, to preserve binary
 *   compatibility. (Also, adding the items is actually not very useful,
 *   since mutability issues prevent an 1 to 1 relationship between arrays
 *   and buffer views.)
 *
 * - Don't use bf_releasebuffer, because it prevents PyArg_ParseTuple("s#", ...
 *   from working. Breaking this would cause several backward compatibility
 *   issues already on Python 2.6.
 *
 * - Behave correctly when array is reshaped in-place, or it's dtype is
 *   altered.
 *

 *************************************************************************/

#include "dtypemeta.h"

/*************************************************************************
 ****************   Implement Buffer Protocol ****************************
 *************************************************************************/

/*************************************************************************
 * PEP 3118 buffer protocol
 *
 * Implementing PEP 3118 is somewhat convoluted because of the desirata:
 *
 * - Don't add new members to ndarray or descr structs, to preserve binary
 *   compatibility. (Also, adding the items is actually not very useful,
 *   since mutability issues prevent an 1 to 1 relationship between arrays
 *   and buffer views.)
 *
 * - Don't use bf_releasebuffer, because it prevents PyArg_ParseTuple("s#", ...
 *   from working. Breaking this would cause several backward compatibility
 *   issues already on Python 2.6.
 *
 * - Behave correctly when array is reshaped in-place, or it's dtype is
 *   altered.
 *
 * The solution taken below is to manually track memory allocated for
 * Py_buffers.
 *************************************************************************/

a few numpy/linalg/lapack_lite directory typos

might be eliminated by regenerating those files with a newer lapack version

however note the following, lapack 3.2.2 was released on June 30, 2010

whereas lapack 3.12.0 (current stable) was released on November 24, 2023

```
$ tail -n 6 numpy/numpy/linalg/lapack_lite/README.rst
The output C files in git use the LAPACK source from the LAPACK_ page, using
version 3.2.2. Unfortunately, newer versions use newer FORTRAN features, which
are increasingly not supported by ``f2c``. As these are found, the patch files
will need to be changed to re-express new constructs with legacy constructs.

.. _LAPACK: https://netlib.org/lapack/index.html
$ 
```

<hr>

Peripheral to the foregoing is noted PowerShell isn't to date a Windows only program.

And the docs aren't specific when commands are provided as to Command Prompt or PowerShell.

The `pyproject.toml` file being updated with some frequency, the following might be considered to implement as being more informative; uppercasing `set` (though cmd is case-insensitive) may emphasise it as a Command Prompt "internal command".

```diff
- # do this instead (which will override this setting)
- # set CIBW_ENVIRONMENT_WINDOWS=PKG_CONFIG_PATH=PWD.replace('\\', '/')
+ # in cmd do this instead (which will override this setting)
+ # SET CIBW_ENVIRONMENT_WINDOWS=PKG_CONFIG_PATH=PWD.replace('\\', '/')

cpython/Doc/library/venv.rst

suggested update (below) changes PowerShell to pwsh

and (as rendered) for pwsh adds POSIX `./ ` and Windows `.\`

```
+-------------+----------+-----------------------------------------------------+
| Platform    | Shell    | Command to activate virtual environment             |
+=============+==========+=====================================================+
| POSIX       | bash/zsh | :samp:`$ source {<venv>}/bin/activate`              |
|             +----------+-----------------------------------------------------+
|             | fish     | :samp:`$ source {<venv>}/bin/activate.fish`         |
|             +----------+-----------------------------------------------------+
|             | csh/tcsh | :samp:`$ source {<venv>}/bin/activate.csh`          |
|             +----------+-----------------------------------------------------+
|             | pwsh     | :samp:`$ ./{<venv>}/bin/Activate.ps1`               |
+-------------+----------+-----------------------------------------------------+
| Windows     | cmd.exe  | :samp:`C:\\> {<venv>}\\Scripts\\activate.bat`       |
|             +----------+-----------------------------------------------------+
|             | pwsh.exe | :samp:`PS C:\\> .\\{<venv>}\\Scripts\\Activate.ps1` |
+-------------+----------+-----------------------------------------------------+

Also check https://github.com/scipy/scipy.org"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-09-18T00:14:34Z","updated_at":"2024-09-20T16:32:45Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The docs have the `pwsh` line executing the activate file instead of the .ps1 file.

```
$ sed -n '331,355p' scipy/doc/source/building/index.rst
    .. tab-set::

      .. tab-item:: Linux
        :sync: linux

        ::

          python -m venv venv
          source venv/bin/activate

      .. tab-item:: macOS
        :sync: macos

        ::

          python -m venv venv
          source venv/bin/activate

      .. tab-item:: Windows
        :sync: windows

        ::

          python -m venv venv
          .\venv\Scripts\activate
$ 
```

doc/source/building/index.rst

```diff
-           .\venv\Scripts\activate
+           .\venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1
```

as per recent output from `pwsh` on Windows

```console
PS C:\Users\bird> python.exe --version
Python 3.12.6
PS C:\Users\bird> python.exe -m venv venv
PS C:\Users\bird> Get-ChildItem venv\Scripts

    Directory: C:\Users\bird\venv\Scripts

Mode                 LastWriteTime         Length Name
----                 -------------         ------ ----
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM           2034 activate
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM            990 activate.bat
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM          26199 Activate.ps1
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM            393 deactivate.bat
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM         108399 pip.exe
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM         108399 pip3.12.exe
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM         108399 pip3.exe
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM         270104 python.exe
-a---           9/17/2024  5:27 PM         258840 pythonw.exe

PS C:\Users\bird>
```

<hr>

Peripheral to the foregoing is noted PowerShell isn't to date a Windows only program.

And the docs aren't specific when commands are provided as to Command Prompt or PowerShell.

The `pyproject.toml` file being updated with some frequency, the following might be considered to implement as being more informative; uppercasing `set` (though cmd.exe is case-insensitive) may emphasise it as a Command Prompt "internal command".

```diff
- # do this instead (which will override this setting)
- # set CIBW_ENVIRONMENT_WINDOWS=PKG_CONFIG_PATH=PWD.replace('\\', '/')
+ # in cmd.exe do this instead (which will override this setting)
+ # SET CIBW_ENVIRONMENT_WINDOWS=PKG_CONFIG_PATH=PWD.replace('\\', '/')
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21576/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21576/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2364103521","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21576#issuecomment-2364103521","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21576","id":2364103521,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6M6Vth","user":{"login":"musvaage","id":112724366,"node_id":"U_kgDOBrgJjg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/112724366?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage","html_url":"https://github.com/musvaage","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-20T16:32:44Z","updated_at":"2024-09-20T16:32:44Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> following CPython

    # concentration
$

dumped directory strings exposed a few typos

scipy/signal/windows/_windows.py

fixed typo in the following and changed to lowercase

```diff
-     The Kaiser window is a very good approximation to the Digital Prolate
-     Spheroidal Sequence, or Slepian window, which is the transform which
+     The Kaiser window is a very good approximation to the discrete prolate
+     spheroidal sequence, or Slepian window, which is the transform which
```

Is this comment correct, i.e collection not a sequence but sequences?

```
$ sed -n '2035,2044p' scipy/scipy/signal/windows/_windows.py
    # Here we want to set up an optimization problem to find a sequence
    # whose energy is maximally concentrated within band [-W,W].
    # Thus, the measure lambda(T,W) is the ratio between the energy within
    # that band, and the total energy. This leads to the eigen-system
    # (A - (l1)I)v = 0, where the eigenvector corresponding to the largest
    # eigenvalue is the sequence with maximally concentrated energy. The
    # collection of eigenvectors of this system are called Slepian
    # sequences, or discrete prolate spheroidal sequences (DPSS). Only the
    # first K, K = 2NW/dt orders of DPSS will exhibit good spectral
    # concentration
$

dumped directory strings exposed a few typos

scipy/signal/windows/_windows.py

fixed typo in the following and changed to lowercase

```diff
-     The Kaiser window is a very good approximation to the Digital Prolate
-     Spheroidal Sequence, or Slepian window, which is the transform which
+     The Kaiser window is a very good approximation to the discrete prolate
+     spheroidal sequence, or Slepian window, which is the transform which
```

Is this comment correct, i.e collection not a sequence but sequences?

```
$ sed -n '2035,2044p' scipy/scipy/signal/windows/_windows.py
    # Here we want to set up an optimization problem to find a sequence
    # whose energy is maximally concentrated within band [-W,W].
    # Thus, the measure lambda(T,W) is the ratio between the energy within
    # that band, and the total energy. This leads to the eigen-system
    # (A - (l1)I)v = 0, where the eigenvector corresponding to the largest
    # eigenvalue is the sequence with maximally concentrated energy. The
    # collection of eigenvectors of this system are called Slepian
    # sequences, or discrete prolate spheroidal sequences (DPSS). Only the
    # first K, K = 2NW/dt orders of DPSS will exhibit good spectral
    # concentration
$
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21617/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21617/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2372069818","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21617#issuecomment-2372069818","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21617","id":2372069818,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6NYum6","user":{"login":"musvaage","id":112724366,"node_id":"U_kgDOBrgJjg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/112724366?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage","html_url":"https://github.com/musvaage","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-24T18:57:37Z","updated_at":"2024-09-24T18:57:37Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> the `sed` output language should be reviewed

dumped directory strings exposed a few typos

scipy/signal/windows/_windows.py

fixed typo in the following and changed to lowercase

```diff
-     The Kaiser window is a very good approximation to the Digital Prolate
-     Spheroidal Sequence, or Slepian window, which is the transform which
+     The Kaiser window is a very good approximation to the discrete prolate
+     spheroidal sequence, or Slepian window, which is the transform which
```

Is this comment correct, i.e collection not a sequence but sequences?

```
$ sed -n '2035,2044p' scipy/scipy/signal/windows/_windows.py
    # Here we want to set up an optimization problem to find a sequence
    # whose energy is maximally concentrated within band [-W,W].
    # Thus, the measure lambda(T,W) is the ratio between the energy within
    # that band, and the total energy. This leads to the eigen-system
    # (A - (l1)I)v = 0, where the eigenvector corresponding to the largest
    # eigenvalue is the sequence with maximally concentrated energy. The
    # collection of eigenvectors of this system are called Slepian
    # sequences, or discrete prolate spheroidal sequences (DPSS). Only the
    # first K, K = 2NW/dt orders of DPSS will exhibit good spectral
    # concentration
$

First of all thank you for responding as I know you're from a different coding community.

Lines 1152 - 1157 from the file include the singular form `sequence`, i.e. `the DPSS window` (a.k.a. …`Slepian window`).

@<!-- -->tylerjereddy made a review of the patch, so please click "Show resolved" so as reveal his review.

Evidently the prior to this patch expanded acronym had `Digital` rather than `Discrete` for `DPSS`.

Line 1851 includes the plural `Sequences` followed by the singular `DPSS.`

> Is this comment correct, i.e collection not a sequence but sequences?

Lines 2035 - 2044 from the file include the following:

`The collection of eigenvectors of this system are called Slepian sequences, or discrete prolate spheroidal sequences (DPSS). Only the first K, K = 2NW/dt orders of DPSS will exhibit good spectral concentration`

If `sequences` and not **sequence** is factually correct then English language convention would not be `DPSS` but **DPSSs**.

Line 2045 provides a reference URL and @<!-- -->tylerjereddy in his review provided an additional reference URL.

<hr>

> not Python objects

Noted from @<!-- -->rkern, but the pure math description might be improved.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2374908017/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

dumped directory strings exposed a few typos

scipy/signal/windows/_windows.py

fixed typo in the following and changed to lowercase

```diff
-     The Kaiser window is a very good approximation to the Digital Prolate
-     Spheroidal Sequence, or Slepian window, which is the transform which
+     The Kaiser window is a very good approximation to the discrete prolate
+     spheroidal sequence, or Slepian window, which is the transform which
```

Is this comment correct, i.e collection not a sequence but sequences?

```
$ sed -n '2035,2044p' scipy/scipy/signal/windows/_windows.py
    # Here we want to set up an optimization problem to find a sequence
    # whose energy is maximally concentrated within band [-W,W].
    # Thus, the measure lambda(T,W) is the ratio between the energy within
    # that band, and the total energy. This leads to the eigen-system
    # (A - (l1)I)v = 0, where the eigenvector corresponding to the largest
    # eigenvalue is the sequence with maximally concentrated energy. The
    # collection of eigenvectors of this system are called Slepian
    # sequences, or discrete prolate spheroidal sequences (DPSS). Only the
    # first K, K = 2NW/dt orders of DPSS will exhibit good spectral
    # concentration
$
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21617/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21617/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2377485374","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21617#issuecomment-2377485374","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21617","id":2377485374,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6NtYw-","user":{"login":"musvaage","id":112724366,"node_id":"U_kgDOBrgJjg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/112724366?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage","html_url":"https://github.com/musvaage","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-26T17:00:56Z","updated_at":"2024-09-26T17:00:56Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Maybe at some point of time this will be revisited in a new Issue.

julia> @edit LogExpFunctions.logaddexp(4, 5) # opened file is read-only (same happens for any other packages)

julia> @edit sin(4) # opened file is NOT read-only. Seems to occur for all base Julia installation files
```

**Expected behavior:** Base files should also be read-only. Seems permissions are not set correctly during installation?

I tested this on Mac (Apple silicon) and Linux (x86). In both cases I installed Julia via `juliaup` (just ran the script `curl -fsSL https://install.julialang.org | sh`).

julia> versioninfo()

With our overlord TIOBE recently saying Julia is at place 20, it is time to go through some Julia tutorials for the win.

Now ... people who want to do this, like me ... go to google search and do a dumb "julia tutorial".

First result I got was:

https://julialang.org/learning/tutorials/

Ok that's good. But these seem to be external links. This is not so good, for various reasons: different
writing style, length and so forth. It just seems a lazy man's listing of ... links. :D

There is also the documentation at:

https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/

But this is not a tutorial.

Would you guys considering an official, updated introduction to Julia, that showcases some things,
in a step-by-step manner? From hello world, to running a commandline script to perhaps more
advanced things, GUI, web-related setting. I am not saying this should be huge, just some teaser
or pointer for people to get started quickly.

I'll go through the external tutorials next, but I usually learn only in "bursts" of, say, 15-20 minutes
and then I have to multi-task something else, so I have a short attention span. This is why a short
official tutorial would be neat - something that can be digested in, say, 20 minutes of being slow
but productive, excluding installing julia (hello world should not take more than, say, 3 minutes
so you'd have 17 more minutes for such a tutorial).

If it is too much work for one person then perhaps a template could be created at the wiki and
filled in slowly by different folks who already know julia. At any rate, please feel free to close
this issue at any moment in time - it is definitely not that important (e. g. compared to bug
reports).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50920/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50920/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1693872806","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50920#issuecomment-1693872806","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50920","id":1693872806,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5k9nKm","user":{"login":"musvaage","id":112724366,"node_id":"U_kgDOBrgJjg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/112724366?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage","html_url":"https://github.com/musvaage","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musvaage/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-08-25T20:05:01Z","updated_at":"2023-08-25T20:05:01Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"@<!-- -->rubyFeedback provided 2nd link to the documentation

permissions

my presumption is `realonly` should be `readonly`

unnecessarily

```
$ grep -nr equivaent Pkg.jl
Pkg.jl/src/Resolve/graphtype.jl:1226:    # number of equivaent classes
$ grep -nr premissions Pkg.jl
Pkg.jl/test/new.jl:169:        # we set realonly here to simulate the premissions in the `$DEPOT/packages` directory
Pkg.jl/test/new.jl:247:        # we set realonly here to simulate the premissions in the `$DEPOT/packages` directory
$ grep -nr uneccesarily Pkg.jl
Pkg.jl/test/new.jl:2476:        # Now we add a commit upstream, if we fetch uneccesarily, we should be able to see it in our clone.
$ 
```

perhaps `resolutionary` could be replaced with another word

```
$ grep -nr resolutionary Pkg.jl
Pkg.jl/src/Operations.jl:1359:    # After downloading resolutionary packages, search for (Julia)Artifacts.toml files

```

$ export PATH=${PATH}:~/.local/bin
$ 
```
